Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Based On Defendant Spoliation Of Evidence
Off Calendar Mar-18-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attys. JENNIFER L. BARRY, ESQ. (Tel No. 858-523-3912) and JAMES K. LYNCH (Tel No. 415-391-0600) of Latham & Watkins LLP appearing for the Plaintiff Lyft, Inc.;
Attys. PETER C. MEIER, ESQ. and BRADFORD K. NEWMAN of Paul Hastings LLP, Tel No. 415-856-7030 appearing for Defendant Travis Vanderzanden.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. As to PLAINTIFF LYFT, INC.'S motion for sanctions based on asserted spoliation of evidence by Mr. VanderZanden is denied in its entirety without prejudice being heard by the trial judge. This motion should be heard by the trial judge rather than a law and motion judge. Because there are disputed factual issues which should be resolved, at least in part, based on live evidence and any rulings made on the motion other than an outright denial of the motion will need to be implemented by the trial judge, the trial judge is in a much better position to decide this motion than a law and motion judge. The parties may wish to request that the case be singly assigned so that the motion might be heard prior to the trial date. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

[Redacted] Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Mar-14-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attys. JENNIFER L. BARRY, ESQ. (Tel No. 858-523-5400) and JAMES K. LYNCH, ESQ. (Tel No. 415-391-0600) of Latham & Watkins LLP appearing for the Plaintiff;
Attys. PETER MEIER, ESQ. and BRADFORD NEWMAN, ESQ. of Paul Hastings LLP, Tel No. 415-856-7030 appearing for Defendant and Cross-Complainant Travis Vanderzanden.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications, and orders as follows: Defendant Travis Vanderzanden's motion for leave to file a second amended cross-complaint is granted on four conditions. The first condition is that Mr. Vanderzanden provide verified responses no later than March 28, 2016 to the following deem-served discovery: 1) state all facts in support of all new allegations in the second amended cross-complaint, 2) state the names of all witnesses with knowledge of those facts and which facts they know, and 3) produce all non-privileged documents relating or referring to those facts. Mr. Vanderzanden will be precluded at trial or in any motion or opposition thereto from introducing any evidence on his cross-claims that is not provided in his discovery responses unless he previously provided that information. The second condition is that discovery will remain open on the cross-claims until the trial date or, if the trial date is continued, 30 days before the new trial date. The third condition is that, if Lyft desires a continuance of the trial date, Mr. Vanderzanden will stipulate to Lyft's request for a trial continuance of no more than four months. The fourth condition is that, if Lyft desires to file a motion for summary judgment or adjudication on the second amended cross-complaint, Mr. Vanderzanden will stipulate that the motion can be heard on 45 days' notice, instead of 75 days' notice. The motion is not untimely. Lyft has not shown that Mr. Vanderzanden should have known in November of 2014 that Lyft was scouring Mr. Vanderzanden's personal dropbox folder. The McCain declaration in support of Lyft's application for a temporary restraining order refers to Mr. Vanderzanden's personal dropbox, but states that Lyft was only looking at Lyft information and not Mr. Vanderzanden's personal information. By early November of 2015, Mr. Vanderzanden was generally aware that Lyft viewed some of the contents of the personal dropbox. Defendant's counsel (Ms. Cheng) complained about the invasion in an e-mail at that time. The extent of the invasion, however, was not clear until the computer report was produced on February 9, 2016. That report describes in detail what was viewed and Mr. Vanderzanden filed this motion shortly thereafter. Lyft's argument that the proposed invasion of privacy claim fails as a matter of law is better addressed on a demurrer than a motion for leave to amend. In all events, a cursory review shows that Lyft's argument may lack merit because in paragraph 52 of its complaint Lyft admits that the personal dropbox was Mr. Vanderzanden's sole property. In addition, paragraph 8 of the McCain declaration in support of Lyft's application for a temporary restraining order acknowledges that the personal dropbox belongs to Mr. Vanderzanden. Even though trial is imminent, the strong policy favoring resolution of claims on their merits fully supports granting this motion. The four conditions on the granting of this motion eliminate any prejudice to Lyft by the granting of the motion. Lyft's demurrer to the 2nd amended cross-complaint may be set on shortened time to be heard April 6, 2016 at 9:30 a.m. in Dept. 302, Law & Motion. Demurrer to be filed by 3/21/16. Opposition to be filed by 3/28/16. Reply to be filed by 4/1/16. Defense counsel shall prepare and submit a proposed order to the Court for signature. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)
